hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (50 mg/100 g) after draining, with a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (207 mg K : 50 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Canned tuna in oil (drained) is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its very low sodium content of 50 mg/100 g and potassium of 207 mg/100 g; drain the oil thoroughly and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; no glycaemic impact; high protein (29.13 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned tuna in oil (drained) is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate or sugar and therefore exerts no glycaemic impact; the high protein content (29.13 g/100 g) promotes satiety, though the higher fat content (8.21 g/100 g) compared to water-packed tuna should be accounted for in total daily energy targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile with omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); low saturated fat (1.54 g/100 g); very low sodium; high selenium and B12
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned tuna in oil (drained) provides a favourable unsaturated fat profile with omega-3 fatty acids that support cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at 1.54 g/100 g, sodium is very low at 50 mg/100 g, and selenium (90 mcg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (2.49 mcg/100 g) contribute to vascular and cardiac function; limit to 2–3 servings per week to manage potential mercury accumulation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) in oil-packed tuna support anti-inflammatory pathways; however, the packing oil type (soybean vs. olive oil) affects net omega-6 to omega-3 ratio
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oil-packed tuna retains more omega-3 fatty acids than brine-packed tuna and may support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; limit to 2–3 servings per week due to potential mercury accumulation, and prefer tuna packed in olive oil over soybean oil to optimise the omega-3 to omega-6 ratio.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned tuna in oil (drained) is a commercially processed fish product made from tuna (typically skipjack or light tuna) packed in vegetable oil and heat-sterilised in a sealed can. After draining the packing oil, the product delivers a firm, slightly flaky texture with a mild, savoury flavour that is richer and more moist than water-packed equivalents. It is one of the most widely consumed canned fish products globally and is a staple pantry ingredient in Singapore, Southeast Asia, Europe, and North America. Per 100 g drained, it provides 198 kcal, 29.13 g protein, 8.21 g fat (predominantly unsaturated, including omega-3 EPA and DHA), 0 g carbohydrate, and only 50 mg sodium — making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate, low-sodium food. The fat content is higher than water-packed canned tuna due to residual absorbed oil. It is suitable for ketogenic, high-protein, and low-sodium dietary patterns. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (zero glycaemic impact), hypertension (very low sodium, favourable potassium ratio), and cardiovascular disease (unsaturated fat profile, omega-3 content, low saturated fat at 1.54 g/100 g). Mercury accumulation is a consideration; consumption should be limited to 2–3 servings per week. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-certified in most commercial forms. Common uses include tuna sandwiches, pasta, salads, rice bowls, and direct consumption.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159) for tuna, light, canned in oil, drained solids. Both are approved sources under Project Veritas v2. Values are consistent between both approved sources. Supplementary micronutrient values (saturated fat, B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are drawn from USDA SR Legacy where HPB does not report them.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159) — canned tuna in oil (drained) contains no carbohydrate by composition. Sugar_g is naturally zero as this is a pure fish and oil product with no added sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175159. Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero as fish contains negligible vitamin C. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this entry, and USDA SR Legacy reports negligible or variable amounts that are not reliably attributed to the drained product specifically; detailed values are not available with sufficient confidence from approved sources as of 2026. Monounsaturated_fat_g (2.86 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.61 g), saturated_fat_g (1.54 g), and additional micronutrients (B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 as HPB does not report these fields. The input description ('Cured and fermented mixed beef and pork sausage') was incorrect and has been replaced with the accurate description for canned tuna in oil (drained). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no GI value exists for this food, consistent with its negligible carbohydrate content. Mercury accumulation is a known concern for tuna; consumption should be limited to 2–3 servings per week, particularly for pregnant women and young children.
